title: Bridge Essay: Assessing prospective and retrospective metacognitive accuracy following traumatic brain injury remotely across cognitive domains.
concept: Metacognition as self-observation — neural substrate of knowing that one knows.

body.text: This bridge paper connects the esoteric/philosophical concept of **Dashboard Diagnosis** with mechanistic science from PubMed/MEDLINE.
**Bridge rationale:** Metacognition — self-awareness, self-monitoring, neural correlates of meta-awareness.
**Phase context:** Metacognition as self-observation — neural substrate of knowing that one knows.

**Assessing prospective and retrospective metacognitive accuracy following traumatic brain injury remotely across cognitive domains.** — Neuropsychol Rehabil (2023). DOI: 10.1080/09602011.2022.2034650. PMID: 35168480. The ability to monitor one's behaviour is frequently impaired following TBI, impacting on patients' rehabilitation. Inaccuracies in judgement or self-reflection of one's performance provides a useful marker of metacognition. However, metacognition is rarely measured during routine neuropsychology assessments and how it varies across cognitive domains is unclear. A cohort of participants consisting of 111 TBI patients [mean age = 45.32(14.15), female = 29] and 84 controls [mean age = 31.51(12.27)

notes: Auto-acquired from PubMed/MEDLINE on 2026-07-12. Phase 1 (Dashboard Diagnosis). Evaluation: Excellent (score=4.5)
